summary: McKenzie shares two simple installer tweaks that significantly improved his software's activation rate. The first is adding a desktop shortcut in addition to a start menu link, which he implemented in 45 seconds. The second is offering to run the program immediately after installation completes. He argues that non-technical users will get distracted or lost if forced to find the program themselves, and that these micro-optimizations doubled the percentage of downloads that resulted in verified program executions.
key_points: Adding a desktop shortcut alongside the start menu link doubled verified execution rates from downloads
Non-technical users have cluttered desktops and will get distracted if not immediately guided to the program
The installer itself is your best tool for getting users into your product — remove every friction point between download and first use
Small, easily-implemented changes (this one took 45 seconds) can have outsized effects on conversion
money_quotes: It also literally doubled the percentage of my downloads which I can verify were executed.
Have you ever seen the desktop of a non-technical user? Its a rather cluttered place.

Don't force them to hunt for your program, because there's a large chance that they'll get distracted away from the task at hand (getting hooked on your product) and will instead resume browsing the Internet, leave the computer and forget they downloaded anything, etc.
So take all the guesswork out of getting to your very best sales vehicle.
Trust me, it will work out much better for the majority of your customers than 'Here's a ZIP file, hope you know what to do', at least if you've got non-technical users like me.
key_insight: Tiny reductions in friction between downloading software and actually running it — like auto-launching after install — can dramatically increase activation and sales.
